questions.text: Which data structure follows LIFO — last-in, first-out?
choices: Queue
Stack
Linked list
Hash table
questions.explanation: A stack removes the most recently added item first — last in, first out. A queue is FIFO, not LIFO.

questions.text: Which pair of Python list operations gives an O(1) push and pop when implementing a stack?
choices: insert(0, x) to push, pop(0) to pop
append(x) to push, pop() to pop
sort() after every push
insert(len(list)//2, x) to push
questions.explanation: Adding/removing at the **end** of a Python list is O(1) (amortised) because no other elements move. Operating on index 0 forces every remaining element to shift, which is O(n).

questions.text: Starting from an empty stack, perform: push(3); push(6); push(1); pop(); pop(); push(9). What value is now on top of the stack?
questions.explanation: push(3)→[3]; push(6)→[3,6]; push(1)→[3,6,1]; pop() removes 1→[3,6]; pop() removes 6→[3]; push(9)→[3,9]. The top is 9.

questions.text: Starting from an empty queue, perform: enqueue(2); enqueue(5); dequeue(); enqueue(7); dequeue(). What value would the *next* dequeue() return?
questions.explanation: enqueue(2)→[2]; enqueue(5)→[2,5]; dequeue() removes the front, 2→[5]; enqueue(7)→[5,7]; dequeue() removes the front, 5→[7]. The only value left, and so the next to leave, is 7.

questions.text: Which Python built-in gives O(1) `append` at one end and O(1) `popleft` at the other, making it the natural choice for a queue (unlike a plain list)?
choices: list
tuple
collections.deque
set
questions.explanation: `collections.deque` supports O(1) operations at both ends. A plain list's `pop(0)` is O(n) because every remaining element has to shift down.

questions.text: You already hold a reference to a node in a singly-linked list and want to insert a new node immediately after it. What is the main advantage over an array-based list here?
choices: Constant-time insertion, with no need to shift any other elements
Constant-time random access by index
It always uses less memory per element than an array
It is always faster to search than an array
questions.explanation: Relinking a couple of `next` references is O(1) regardless of list length. An array would need every following element shifted along to make room.

questions.text: Which of these statements about singly-linked lists are true?
choices: Each node stores a reference to the next node
Traversal from the head to a given node is O(1) in general
Deleting the head node is O(1)
Random access by index (e.g. the 5th element) is O(1)
questions.explanation: Nodes do store a `next` reference, and removing the head is just moving the `head` reference to `head.next`, O(1). But reaching an arbitrary node — whether by traversal or by index — requires walking from the head, which is O(n), not O(1).

questions.text: An in-order traversal of a binary search tree always visits the nodes in ______ order. (One word.)
questions.explanation: In-order visits left subtree, then the node, then right subtree. Combined with the BST property (left < node < right at every node), this always produces the values in sorted, ascending order.

questions.text: A binary search tree is built, starting empty, by inserting 5, 3, 8, 1, 4, 7, 9 in that order. Taking the root's depth as 0, what is the depth of the node containing 4?
questions.explanation: 5 becomes the root (depth 0). 3 < 5 goes left of 5 (depth 1). 4 < 5 goes left to 3, then 4 > 3 goes right of 3 (depth 2). So the node containing 4 is at depth 2.

questions.text: Using the same BST (built by inserting 5, 3, 8, 1, 4, 7, 9 in that order), write its pre-order traversal as comma-separated values with no spaces, e.g. "1,2,3".
questions.explanation: Pre-order visits the node itself, then its left subtree, then its right subtree: 5, then (3, 1, 4) from the left subtree, then (8, 7, 9) from the right subtree — giving 5,3,1,4,8,7,9.

questions.text: A well-designed hash table gives O(1) average-case lookup mainly because…
choices: it keeps all of its keys in sorted order
a good hash function spreads keys evenly across buckets, so each bucket holds only a few entries
it never has any collisions
it doubles in size before every single insertion
questions.explanation: Even spreading keeps each bucket's chain short, so locating a key inside its bucket takes roughly constant time on average. Collisions still happen; the hash table's design just keeps their impact small.

questions.text: Which of these are valid ways to handle a hash collision (two keys hashing to the same index)?
choices: Chaining: store multiple entries in a list at that bucket
Open addressing: probe forward to the next free slot
Ignore the second key and silently discard its value
Refuse to insert anything else into the table ever again
questions.explanation: Chaining and open addressing (e.g. linear probing) are the standard collision-resolution strategies. Silently dropping data or refusing all further inserts would make the table unusable.

questions.text: Using the hash function hash(key) = (sum of the ASCII/character codes of its characters) mod 5, what index does the key "dog" map to? (ord('d')=100, ord('o')=111, ord('g')=103.)
